content:
# Loop Control Statements

Control statements alter the sequential execution of a program.

*Examples*

-   **if-elif-else**
-   **while, for**
-   **break, continue**

## Break

Break statement makes the program exit a loop early.

![](https://res.cloudinary.com/learning-platform/image/fetch/dpr_auto,w_auto,f_auto,q_auto/https://nkb-backend-ccbp-media-static.s3.ap-south-1.amazonaws.com/prod/media/profile_pic/card-notes-attachment/fb287ad2-ebb8-4d6f-b76e-3391d8c51d4e.png)

## Using Break

Generally, break is used to exit a loop when a condition is satisfied.
  ![](https://res.cloudinary.com/learning-platform/image/fetch/dpr_auto,w_auto,f_auto,q_auto/https://nkb-backend-ccbp-media-static.s3.ap-south-1.amazonaws.com/prod/media/profile_pic/card-notes-attachment/9d31dfba-d068-4bd6-a111-aa90d4310cda.png)

In the below example, when the variable `i` value equals to `3` the `break` statement gets executed and stops the execution of the loop further.

#### Code

```python
for i in range(5):
  if i == 3:
    break
  print(i)
print("END")
```

#### Output

```
0
1
2
END
```

### Break in Nested Loop

Break in inner loop stops the execution of the inner loop.

![](https://res.cloudinary.com/learning-platform/image/fetch/dpr_auto,w_auto,f_auto,q_auto/https://nkb-backend-ccbp-media-static.s3.ap-south-1.amazonaws.com/prod/media/profile_pic/card-notes-attachment/998c6988-c47f-4762-89ce-06007436aba3.png)

## Continue

Continue makes the program skip the remaining statements in the current iteration and begin the next iteration.

![](https://res.cloudinary.com/learning-platform/image/fetch/dpr_auto,w_auto,f_auto,q_auto/https://nkb-backend-ccbp-media-static.s3.ap-south-1.amazonaws.com/prod/media/profile_pic/card-notes-attachment/047babd7-7cb8-4c1a-9eec-ccc5940e583e.png)

### Using Continue

Generally, continue is used to skip the remaining statements in the current iteration when a condition is satisfied.

  

![](https://res.cloudinary.com/learning-platform/image/fetch/dpr_auto,w_auto,f_auto,q_auto/https://nkb-backend-ccbp-media-static.s3.ap-south-1.amazonaws.com/prod/media/profile_pic/card-notes-attachment/fa8a8b5c-9f02-4cf7-9d31-3c278bb19b77.png)

In the below example, when the variable `i` value equals to `3` the next statements in the loop body are skipped.

#### Code

```python
for i in range(5):
  if i == 3:
    continue
  print(i)
print("END")
```

#### Output

```
0
1
2
4
END
```

## Pass

Pass statement is used as a syntactic placeholder. When it is executed, nothing happens.

Generally used when we have to test the code before writing the complete code.

![](https://res.cloudinary.com/learning-platform/image/fetch/dpr_auto,w_auto,f_auto,q_auto/https://nkb-backend-ccbp-media-static.s3.ap-south-1.amazonaws.com/prod/media/profile_pic/card-notes-attachment/2a631d0f-b95d-42dc-ba55-b80eda1a1ee7.png)

### Empty Loops

We can use pass statements to test code written so far, before writing loop logic.

![](https://res.cloudinary.com/learning-platform/image/fetch/dpr_auto,w_auto,f_auto,q_auto/https://nkb-backend-ccbp-media-static.s3.ap-south-1.amazonaws.com/prod/media/profile_pic/card-notes-attachment/a29bea25-3b7f-43e6-bfa1-cea157432c29.png)
